frameworks.name: decision by sampling
frameworks.one_line: Subjective value equals rank within a memory-retrieved sample of attribute values

frameworks.name: efficient coding
frameworks.one_line: Provides a normative justification for the DbS rank transformation

frameworks.name: range-frequency theory
frameworks.one_line: Closely connected to the noisy-channel generalization of DbS

frameworks.name: classic stable-preference theory
frameworks.one_line: Cannot account for the context dependence DbS explains

core_question: Can efficient coding normatively justify decision by sampling and extend it to noisy, finite-sample regimes?
key_assumptions: Subjective value derives from rank in a memory-retrieved sample
Efficient representation in a noiseless channel yields a uniform response distribution
Cognitive limitations make decision samples finite and thus noisy
Efficient coding in a noisy channel requires signal smoothing
manipulations: Contrasted noiseless versus noisy communication-channel regimes
Varied the number of available response categories
Compared classic stable-preference accounts against DbS-based accounts
key_findings: Rank transformation implements the uniform output distribution required by noiseless efficient coding
A new smoothed generalization of DbS emerges from noisy-channel efficient coding
The generalized DbS captures how context sensitivity varies with the number of response categories
The generalized account aligns closely with range-frequency theory
interpretations: DbS is a normatively efficient strategy under capacity and sample-size constraints
Context-dependent valuation can be derived from efficient coding rather than stipulated
